description: GAP-001 spike review — 10 gap items, all design decisions captured before v1.1 roadmap is written

sections.title: 1 — Scale Ratio Input (GAP-T2-00)
sections.summary: Add a 1:100 / 1:50 / 1:200 input path so estimators don't need to draw a calibration line when the title block shows the scale.

questions.title: Where does ratio input live?
questions.context: CalibrationDialog.tsx is the existing modal (shown after drawing two points). It currently has a single flow: line length → real-world distance → confirm. Adding a ratio mode means either extending this dialog or adding a separate entry point.

options.label: New tab in CalibrationDialog
options.description: Add a 'Type ratio' tab alongside the existing 'Draw line' tab. User picks the mode before interacting with the canvas. Keeps all calibration in one place.

options.label: Separate toolbar button
options.description: A new 'Type 1:N' button in the ribbon that opens a lightweight popover — just a ratio input + confirm. Drawn-line path unchanged. Cleaner separation.

options.label: Add ratio field inside existing CalibrationDialog
options.description: After drawing the line and entering the real-world distance, also show a 'Or type a scale ratio' field. Either field can produce the calibration. Most compact.

questions.title: What ratio formats to accept?
questions.context: Construction drawings use '1:100', '1:50', '1:200'. Some older Australian drawings also use '1:25' or '1:500'. The math is pixelsPerMm = pageWidthPx / (physicalWidthMm × N) where N is the denominator.

options.label: "1:N" only
options.description: Accept only the standard construction notation (e.g. '1:100'). Reject '100' or '0.01'. Simple validation, clear error message.

options.label: "1:N" and bare number
options.description: Accept '1:100' or '100' (treated as 1:100). Easier to type. Small risk of ambiguity.

options.label: Smart parse any form
options.description: Accept '1:100', '100', '1/100', '0.01'. Most flexible but more validation code.

questions.title: Physical page size source
questions.context: To convert a ratio to pixelsPerMm, the app needs the physical sheet size (e.g. A1 = 841mm wide). PDF.js provides page.view = [0, 0, widthPt, heightPt] in user-space points (72 pts/inch). A1 at 72dpi = 2384pt wide → 841mm. This conversion is reliable for PDFs generated from CAD. Scanned PDFs may have wrong metadata.

options.label: Auto-derive from PDF page.view
options.description: Use PDF.js page dimensions (pts → mm via 25.4/72). Works for any CAD-exported PDF. Scanned PDFs may be wrong — show the derived sheet size for user confirmation.

options.label: User picks sheet size from a dropdown
options.description: Offer A0/A1/A2/A3/A4 and common US sizes. User selects which sheet size matches the plan. Reliable but adds one more step.

options.label: User draws one known edge
options.description: Prompt user to draw along a sheet edge of known length (e.g. draw the full width and type 841). Accurate but more work than typing a ratio.

sections.title: 2 — Markup Vertex Editing (GAP-T1-01)
sections.summary: Let estimators drag individual vertices of a committed linear, area, perimeter, or wall markup to correct mistakes without redrawing the whole shape.

questions.title: How does vertex edit mode activate?
questions.context: Currently, clicking a committed markup selects it (selection ring appears). Double-click is used during drawing to finish a shape, but has no post-commit role. Right-click opens the context menu (MarkupContextMenu.tsx with Edit, Recolor Group, Delete).

options.label: Double-click the committed markup
options.description: Double-click (post-commit, different from double-click-to-finish which is pre-commit) enters vertex edit mode. Natural discovery — users try this first. No new UI element needed.

options.label: Right-click → 'Edit vertices'
options.description: Add 'Edit vertices' to the existing context menu. Discoverable through the same menu the user already uses for Edit/Recolor/Delete.

options.label: Click to select then click again
options.description: First click selects (existing behavior). Second click on the selected markup enters vertex edit mode. Simple, no extra menu item. Risk: accidental double-click.

questions.title: Vertex handle appearance
questions.context: HoverRing.tsx and the selection ring pattern already exist. Konva Circle nodes at each vertex would be the handle. The page-space inverse transform (stage.getAbsoluteTransform().copy().invert().point()) is established for converting screen coords to page coords during drag.

options.label: Small filled squares (CAD-style)
options.description: 8px × 8px white squares with a colored border at each vertex. Classic CAD convention — every estimator who has used AutoCAD or Bluebeam will recognize this.

options.label: Small circles matching markup color
options.description: Filled circles (radius 6-8px) at each vertex, colored to match the markup's color with white outline. Consistent with the existing count pin circle visual language.

options.label: Orange accent handles
options.description: Orange handles regardless of markup color — same orange as the chain mode badge (#f97316). Makes handles visually distinct from the markup itself. Clear 'editing mode active' signal.

questions.title: How to exit vertex edit mode
questions.context: Existing pattern from CalibrationDialog cancel: user can retry immediately. MarkupNamePopup commits on Enter or clicking Confirm. The selection ring disappears on click-off (clearSelection in handleStageClick). All vertex moves should be wrapped in EditMarkupCommand (already exists in markupStore) so Ctrl+Z undoes the whole edit session.

options.label: Enter saves, Escape cancels
options.description: Keyboard-first: Enter commits all vertex changes, Escape restores original positions. Consistent with markup name popup and scale calibration patterns.

options.label: Click anywhere outside the markup
options.description: Clicking off the markup auto-commits vertex positions (same as clicking off a text field). No keyboard required. Risk: accidental commits when clicking to pan.

options.label: Floating Done / Cancel buttons near markup
options.description: Small inline buttons appear near the markup bounding box: [Done] [Cancel]. Most explicit — can't accidentally commit. Adds a UI element to manage.

sections.title: 3 — Markup Translate / Move (GAP-T1-02)
sections.summary: Let estimators drag a committed markup to a new position — a count pin to the right room, a polygon to the correct slab.

questions.title: How does drag-to-move trigger?
questions.context: CanvasViewport already has isDraggable on the Stage for pan (middle mouse button). The rubber-band fix added a 4px movement threshold (rubberBandDraggedRef) to distinguish click-to-select from drag. The same mechanism can gate markup drag. The markup Group nodes currently have listening=true in Layer 1b.

options.label: Drag a selected markup to move it
options.description: After clicking to select a markup, dragging it (with the same LMB movement threshold) moves it. Natural for anyone who has used Bluebeam or Powerpoint.

options.label: Hold Shift while dragging
options.description: Shift+drag moves the markup. Without Shift, LMB drag continues to mean rubber-band select (or do nothing on a selected markup). Avoids accidental moves.

options.label: Dedicated 'Move' mode in the ribbon
options.description: A Move tool button in the ribbon. While active, clicking+dragging any markup moves it. Clear but requires tool switching.

questions.title: Group move — do multiple selected markups move together?
questions.context: Rubber-band multi-select already works and stores selectedMarkupIds in viewerStore. Moving a group would offset all selected markup coordinates by the same delta. This is technically straightforward but adds complexity to the undo command (a batch MoveMarkupsCommand vs a single MoveMarkupCommand).

options.label: Yes — drag any selected markup, all selected move
options.description: Standard behavior in every graphics tool. Works for rubber-band selections too. Requires a batch undo command.

options.label: Single-markup move only for v1.1
options.description: Only the dragged markup moves. Other selections are deselected. Simpler implementation. Group move can be added later.

questions.title: What is the drag movement threshold?
questions.context: The rubber-band pattern uses 4px before activating (prevents accidental drag on a precise click). A markup drag-to-move should also have a threshold to prevent accidentally moving a markup when trying to click-to-select it.

options.label: Same 4px as rubber-band
options.description: Reuse the existing constant. Consistent across all drag operations in the app.

options.label: Larger — 8-12px
options.description: A slightly larger threshold means you need more deliberate movement to trigger a move. Safer for estimators who click precisely on small markups.

sections.title: 4 — Pricing Column in BOQ (GAP-T1-03)
sections.summary: Attach a unit rate to each item name so the BOQ shows Rate ($/unit) and Cost ($) columns alongside Quantity — turning measurement output into a priced Bill of Quantities.

questions.title: Where does the estimator enter a unit rate?
questions.context: TotalsRow.tsx currently has a Lightbulb slot (visibility toggle) and a '+' slot (arm from totals). MarkupNamePopup.tsx has name/category/color/wall-height fields. The rates dictionary could live in projectStore as rates: Record<string, number> (same additive pattern as hiddenItemNames).

options.label: Inline in the totals panel rate cell
options.description: Each totals row shows a rate field (e.g. '$___/m²'). Click it to edit inline. Live feedback as rate × qty = cost updates immediately. No modal needed.

options.label: In the MarkupNamePopup when naming a markup
options.description: Add a Rate field alongside Name/Category/Color. Rate is set when first naming the item. Can be changed via Edit markup later.

options.label: Dedicated Rates tab or panel
options.description: A separate pricing screen (e.g. in the Estimating ribbon tab) that lists all item names with rate fields. Separates 'measuring' from 'pricing' workflows.

questions.title: Rate granularity — what does a rate apply to?
questions.context: boq-aggregator.ts groups markups by item name across all pages. A 'rates' Record<string, number> keyed by item name is the simplest model. Edge case: same item name with different UoM on different pages (e.g. 'brick' as count on one page, linear on another) — would need two rates.

options.label: Per item name (e.g. 'Concrete Slab' → $X per m²)
options.description: One rate per name, applied to the aggregated quantity. Simple. Works for 99% of real takeoff data. Warn if the same name has multiple UoM.

options.label: Per item name + UoM pair
options.description: 'Concrete Slab @ m²' → $X; 'Concrete Slab @ m' → $Y. Handles the edge case cleanly but makes the rate key more complex.

questions.title: BOQ export format with pricing
questions.context: Current export: Item | Quantity | UoM. boq-writers.ts builds this in main process using ExcelJS. Adding pricing means new columns. The XLSX has frozen title row + colored category headers already.

options.label: Add Rate and Cost columns after Quantity
options.description: Item | Quantity | UoM | Rate ($/unit) | Cost ($). Standard priced BOQ layout. Grand total Cost row at the bottom.

options.label: Cost column only (hide rate)
options.description: Item | Quantity | UoM | Cost ($). Cleaner output — rate is internal. Less useful if client wants to verify unit rates.

options.label: Separate 'Priced BOQ' sheet in XLSX
options.description: Keep the existing quantity sheet unchanged; add a second sheet with the priced layout. Both always exported. Maximally compatible with existing workflows.

questions.title: Currency symbol and formatting
questions.context: No currency handling exists today. ExcelJS can apply numFmt to cells (e.g. '$#,##0.00'). Currency choice affects both the totals panel display and the XLSX export.

options.label: No currency symbol — user adds in Excel
options.description: Keep it unitless: show '12500.00' not '$12,500.00'. Avoids currency assumptions. User formats in Excel as needed.

options.label: Default AUD with settings option
options.description: Show '$' by default. Settings tab lets user pick currency symbol (AUD / USD / NZD / GBP / EUR / custom). Stored in localStorage clmc.ui (never in .clmc).

options.label: Currency per project in .clmc file
options.description: Currency is a project-level setting (like hiddenItemNames). Allows different projects to use different currencies. Slightly more complex persistence.

sections.title: 5 — Item Library (GAP-T1-05)
sections.summary: A persistent dictionary of item names (and optionally rates) that survives across projects — so estimators don't retype 'Concrete Slab 100mm' or '2400mm Stud Wall' every session.

questions.title: Where does the library live?
questions.context: Electron gives access to app.getPath('userData') — a per-machine AppData folder. A JSON file there (e.g. clmc-library.json) persists across all projects. Alternatively, embed the library in each .clmc file. The hidden-items pattern used additive optional fields in the project schema with no formatVersion bump.

options.label: Global — AppData/clmc-library.json
options.description: One library for all projects on this machine. Add an item once; it appears in every project's autocomplete. Standard approach (e.g. AutoCAD tool palettes).

options.label: Per-project — embedded in .clmc
options.description: Library travels with the project file. Open the same .clmc on another machine and the library is there. No cross-project sharing.

options.label: Both — global library + per-project overrides
options.description: Global base library; per-project additions. Most powerful but most complex to manage (merge logic, conflict resolution).

questions.title: How does the estimator add to the library?
questions.context: CategoryAutocomplete.tsx already shows completions from current project item names. MarkupNamePopup.tsx is the primary naming UI. A 'Save to library' action fits naturally as a button in either place.

options.label: 'Save to library' checkbox/button in MarkupNamePopup
options.description: After filling in name/category/color in the popup, a 'Save to library' checkbox appears. Check it and the item is persisted to the global library on confirm.

options.label: Right-click on totals row → 'Add to library'
options.description: After placing a markup, right-click its row in the totals panel to save the name/category to the library. Fits the existing TotalsRowContextMenu pattern.

options.label: Library management screen in Settings tab
options.description: Settings tab (currently a stub) gets a 'Library' section: view/add/delete/edit library items. Explicit management, no implicit saves.

questions.title: Does the library store rates?
questions.context: If Pricing (GAP-T1-03) is also built, the item library can store {name, category, defaultColor, defaultRate}. When an estimator picks a library item, the rate auto-fills. This tightly couples the two phases.

options.label: Yes — library stores name + category + color + rate
options.description: One-time setup: add item to library with its typical rate. Every new project starts with rates pre-filled. Requires Pricing phase to be built first or in parallel.

options.label: No — library stores name + category + color only
options.description: Keep the library simple in v1.1. Rates stay per-project. Library can gain rate support in a future phase.

sections.title: 6 — Markup Notes / Comments (GAP-T2-03)
sections.summary: Attach a free-text note to a markup or item group (e.g. 'check with structural — may be load bearing'). Notes appear in tooltips and optionally in the BOQ.

questions.title: Note attachment level — per placement or per item name?
questions.context: BaseMarkup in markup.ts can gain a notes?: string field (additive, no formatVersion bump needed — same pattern as hiddenItemNames). Item-level notes would live in the rates/library store. Both have different persistence implications.

options.label: Per individual markup placement
options.description: Each placed count pin, polyline, polygon can have its own note. Most granular — 'this specific window is a custom size'. Stored in BaseMarkup.

options.label: Per item name group
options.description: 'All instances of Concrete Slab share one note.' Simpler to read in the BOQ. Stored in the project rates dictionary or library.

options.label: Both — group note with per-placement override
options.description: Group note as default; individual overrides possible. Most flexible. More complex UI.

questions.title: Where do notes appear once added?
questions.context: MarkupTooltip.tsx shows name, category, measurement on hover. TotalsRow.tsx shows the row. boq-writers.ts builds the XLSX export. Notes could surface in any or all of these.

options.label: Hover tooltip only
options.description: Notes shown in the canvas hover tooltip. Low impact on the BOQ layout. Good for quick QA during placement.

options.label: Tooltip + note icon badge on canvas + BOQ Notes column
options.description: Full visibility: badge on the markup shows a note exists; tooltip shows the text; XLSX export gets a Notes column. Professional — matches Bluebeam's comment system.

options.label: Tooltip + totals panel (truncated)
options.description: Notes shown in the hover tooltip and as small italic text under the item name in the totals panel. No BOQ export change.

sections.title: 7 — Cross-Page Item Breakdown (GAP-T2-04)
sections.summary: Show which pages contribute to each BOQ item — e.g., 'Concrete Slab: 120.5 m² total → Page 1: 45 m², Page 3: 75.5 m²'.

questions.title: Where does breakdown appear in the totals panel?
questions.context: TotalsCategoryBlock.tsx → TotalsRow.tsx is the current hierarchy. boq-aggregator.ts already walks per-page (it skips uncalibrated pages). Adding pageBreakdown: {pageIndex, qty}[] to BoqItemRow is straightforward.

options.label: Collapsible sub-rows under each item in the totals panel
options.description: Click an item row to expand page-level sub-rows: ↳ Page 1: 45 m², ↳ Page 3: 75.5 m². Same pattern as a tree view. Inline, no extra UI.

options.label: Separate 'By Page' tab in the totals panel header
options.description: The TotalsPanelHeader gets a second tab. 'Summary' shows current view; 'By Page' shows a page-grouped breakdown. Cleaner separation but adds navigation.

options.label: XLSX export only — no panel UI change
options.description: Add a 'By Page' sheet to the Excel export only. Totals panel stays simple. Estimators who need the breakdown open the export.

questions.title: BOQ export — how is breakdown shown in the .xlsx?
questions.context: Current XLSX: one sheet, one row per item, colored category headers. A breakdown could be indented sub-rows, a second sheet, or an extra column with a page list string.

options.label: Indented sub-rows in the main sheet
options.description: Under each item row: indented rows for each contributing page. Simple, no sheet switching.

options.label: Separate 'By Page' worksheet
options.description: Second worksheet in the same workbook: Item | Page | Qty per row. Keeps the main sheet clean.

options.label: Both — main sheet sub-rows + 'By Page' sheet
options.description: Most complete. Larger XLSX. Most useful for QA and client delivery.

sections.title: 8 — Snap to Existing Geometry (GAP-T2-05)
sections.summary: During markup placement, snap the cursor to nearby existing markup vertices so points connect precisely — reducing accumulated measurement error.

questions.title: What can the cursor snap to?
questions.context: Page-space coords are available for all placed markups (stored as normalized 0–1). On mousemove, scan existing markup vertex positions within a snap radius. The stage inverse transform pattern (canonical in STATE.md) handles screen → page-space conversion at any zoom.

options.label: Existing markup vertices only
options.description: Snap to the exact corner points of placed polylines and polygons. Simplest. Allows chains of markups to share exact endpoints.

options.label: Vertices + edge midpoints
options.description: Also snap to the midpoint of each markup segment. Useful for measuring from the center of a wall.

options.label: Vertices + midpoints + grid (user-definable spacing)
options.description: Full snap grid option. Most powerful but most configuration surface. Probably v1.2 scope.

questions.title: Snap on/off default and toggle
questions.context: Snap changes cursor behavior globally during markup placement. Some estimators will always want it; others find it frustrating when clicking near but not on a snap point. A toolbar toggle or keyboard shortcut (S) would cover both needs.

options.label: On by default, S key toggles
options.description: Snap is active whenever a markup tool is armed. Press S to temporarily disable. Persists in localStorage (like panel widths). Most useful out of the box.

options.label: Off by default, toolbar button enables
options.description: Opt-in. Users who want it turn it on. Avoids surprising first-time users who don't understand why the cursor is jumping.

options.label: On by default, hold Alt to momentarily disable
options.description: Snap is default-on; holding Alt suspends it for one placement. No persistent toggle. Good for occasional override without interrupting flow.

sections.title: 9 — Installer & Auto-Update (GAP-T2-06)
sections.summary: Build and distribute a proper Windows installer (.exe) so estimators can install the app from a file — and auto-update when new versions are released.

questions.title: Distribution and update channel
questions.context: electron-builder is already configured in package.json. electron-updater reads a RELEASES JSON from a URL. GitHub Releases is free, public, and integrates with Actions CI. A self-hosted server gives more control but requires infra. The app is Windows-only.

options.label: GitHub Releases
options.description: Free, zero infrastructure. electron-updater polls the GitHub release API. New version = create a GitHub Release and upload the installer + RELEASES.json. Standard approach for Electron indie apps.

options.label: Self-hosted server (S3 or VPS)
options.description: Full control over the release feed, access control, analytics. More setup. Required if the app is distributed privately (not public GitHub repo).

options.label: Start with GitHub Releases, migrate if needed
options.description: Ship now. Move to self-hosted if the repo needs to be private or access control is required.

questions.title: Auto-update notification behavior
questions.context: electron-updater has three modes: (1) silent background download + restart-to-apply notification; (2) prompt user to download+install; (3) user-triggered via Help → Check for Updates. The estimating context means users open the app before a site visit — a surprise restart prompt could be disruptive.

options.label: Silent download, non-disruptive notification
options.description: Download happens in background. When done, a small banner/toast says 'Update ready — restart when you're done.' User decides when to restart. Least disruptive.

options.label: Prompt on launch if update available
options.description: Check for update at startup. If available, show a dialog: 'A new version is available. Install now?' Yes restarts+updates, No skips. User always knows about updates.

options.label: Manual only — Help → Check for Updates
options.description: No automatic behavior. Estimator is always in control. Simplest implementation; updates won't be applied unless user actively checks.

questions.title: Installer format and code signing
questions.context: electron-builder can produce NSIS .exe (recommended for Windows), portable .exe (no install), or .msi. Code signing requires a certificate. Without signing, Windows Defender SmartScreen blocks the installer for unknown publishers (user has to click 'More info → Run anyway'). An EV cert costs ~$300–400/yr and eliminates SmartScreen. A standard OV cert costs less but still triggers SmartScreen on first run.

options.label: NSIS .exe, no code signing for v1.1
options.description: Ship unsigned. SmartScreen will warn first-time users. Acceptable during internal/beta testing. Add signing before distributing to clients.

options.label: NSIS .exe + portable .exe, no signing
options.description: Same as above but also offer a portable version (no install required). Useful for machines where the user can't install software.

options.label: NSIS .exe with EV code signing
options.description: Eliminate the SmartScreen barrier. Required before any client distribution. Costs ~$300–400/yr.

sections.title: 10 — PDF with Markups Export (GAP-T1-04)
sections.summary: Export a flattened PDF showing the estimator's markups baked onto the plan — for delivery to clients and colleagues who don't have the .clmc file.

questions.title: Include in v1.1 or defer to v2.0?
questions.context: The spike rated this as high value but high complexity (~5+ plans). pdf-lib can draw shapes onto a PDFDocument, but coordinate transform between Konva page-space (normalized 0–1) and PDF page-space (pts) is non-trivial, especially across multi-page projects with different page sizes. The other 9 gaps are lower-risk and higher immediate ROI.

options.label: Defer to v2.0
options.description: Focus v1.1 on the 9 higher-ROI gaps. PDF export is a full phase on its own — tackle it after the UX foundations are solid.

options.label: Include in v1.1 after other items
options.description: Add it at the end of the v1.1 build sequence. Adds ~5 plans and significant complexity.

options.label: Include in v1.1 as a priority
options.description: Move it up the sequence. This is the most requested feature by clients who want to see the takeoff work.

sections.title: F — Post-Commit Step-Level Undo (Phase 10 extension)
sections.summary: Phase 10 gave Ctrl+Z point-level granularity during in-progress drawing. But once a markup is committed (finished), Ctrl+Z still removes the whole shape in one step. Should it re-open the shape for continued editing instead?

questions.title: What should Ctrl+Z do after a committed multi-point markup?
questions.context: Phase 10 (completed 2026-05-19) handles the in-progress case: Ctrl+Z during drawing pops the last unfinished point. After committing (pressing Enter or double-clicking to finish a linear/area/perimeter/wall), the current behavior is that Ctrl+Z removes the entire shape as a single undo step via RemoveMarkupCommand. The question is whether 'committed' should be re-enterable by undo.

options.label: Keep current: whole-shape undo (one Ctrl+Z = whole markup gone)
options.description: Standard command-pattern behavior. Predictable: one placement = one undo. The shape is not recoverable by Ctrl+Y after removal unless Ctrl+Y is pressed immediately. Simple and consistent with the existing 50-level undo stack.

options.label: Re-open in drawing mode (undo the commit, keep all points)
options.description: Ctrl+Z after committing puts the shape back into in-progress drawing mode with all placed points intact. The estimator can then add more points, pop points with Ctrl+Z, or re-commit with Enter. Most powerful — no geometry is ever permanently lost on the first Ctrl+Z after finishing.

options.label: Re-open in drawing mode AND pop the last point
options.description: Ctrl+Z after committing re-enters drawing mode with one fewer point (as if you had pressed Ctrl+Z once while still drawing). Consistent mental model: each Ctrl+Z always removes one point, whether or not the shape was committed.

questions.title: If the markup re-opens in drawing mode after undo, how does the UX communicate this?
questions.context: Relevant only if option (b) or (c) is chosen for Q-30. The risk: after Ctrl+Z the canvas looks similar to mid-drawing but the user may not realise they are now back in an active drawing session. The useMarkupTool state machine already has mode:'drawing' — it would need to be re-entered from outside the normal tool-arm flow.

options.label: Same as normal in-progress drawing — no extra signal
options.description: The shape snaps back to in-progress appearance (dashed/open line, vertex handles, placement cursor). Experienced users recognise this immediately. Minimal UI change.

options.label: Brief toast: 'Shape re-opened — continue drawing or press Enter to commit'
options.description: A ConfirmationToast (existing component) surfaces for 3 seconds. Ensures first-time users understand what happened. Follows the existing toast pattern (parent-owned lifecycle).

options.label: Animated transition — shape 'unfolds' from committed to in-progress state
options.description: A short visual animation signals the state change clearly. More complex to implement. May be distracting during fast editing.

sections.title: 11 — Build Sequence & Priorities
sections.summary: Decide the order and scope of v1.1 phases. The spike ranked 10 items by impact/effort — now confirm which to build and in what order.

questions.title: What is the first v1.1 phase?
questions.context: The spike recommended Scale Ratio Input first (#1 by ROI). But installer/auto-update (GAP-T2-06) is the blocker for any real-world distribution. Vertex editing (T1-01) is the biggest UX unlock. The sequence is a strategic choice.

options.label: Scale ratio input (1:100 mode) — highest ROI per effort
options.description: 3× faster calibration for every plan. Immediate daily-use benefit. Low risk. ~2 plans.

options.label: Installer + auto-update — enables real-world testing
options.description: Distribute to clients and collect real feedback before building more features. ~2–3 plans. No feature work.

options.label: Vertex editing — biggest UX unlock
options.description: Removes the biggest frustration: frozen geometry. Makes demos more impressive. ~3 plans.

questions.title: Vertex Editing + Translate — one phase or two?
questions.context: Vertex editing (T1-01) and translate/move (T1-02) both involve post-commit geometry manipulation and share the same Konva drag infrastructure. Building them together saves a research/planning cycle. Building separately lets you ship vertex editing sooner.

options.label: One phase — vertex editing + translate together
options.description: Shared infrastructure, single research pass, one phase closure. ~4–5 plans.

options.label: Two phases — vertex editing first, translate follow-up
options.description: Ship vertex editing sooner. Translate builds on the same foundation with a lighter follow-up phase.

options.label: Translate only (skip vertex editing for v1.1)
options.description: Move is simpler to implement. Vertex editing is more complex. Defer vertex editing to v2.0.

questions.title: Pricing + Item Library — one phase or two?
questions.context: Pricing column (T1-03) adds rates to the project schema and BOQ export. Item library (T1-05) adds a persistent AppData dictionary. They overlap if the library stores rates (Q-16). Building together makes sense if library stores rates; separately otherwise.

options.label: One phase — pricing + library together (library stores rates)
options.description: The library is the rate master — enter a rate once, reuse across projects. Tightly coupled, ~6–7 plans.

options.label: Two phases — pricing first, library adds names+rates later
options.description: Pricing in one phase (per-project rates). Library in a follow-up (global name+rate persistence). More modular.

options.label: Library first (names only), pricing adds rates later
options.description: Ship name autocomplete library without rates. Pricing phase adds rates to both project and library. Two clear milestones.
